A cooperative platform with 2,132 innovations, 580 database tables, 139 edge functions, and an archive of over 10,000 files has a problem: no single AI agent can hold all of it in memory at once. The Librarian MCP solves this today — 25 tools, 15 index files, 24-second rebuild. But the archive is growing faster than the Librarian can efficiently serve.

The Fingertips System is the answer: a self-scaling knowledge infrastructure where Librarian instances automatically reproduce when data volume exceeds their capacity. More data, more Librarians — the same way more people means more congressional representatives.

# Pudding #102 — The Blizzard

*The most dangerous failure is the one that looks like nothing.*

---

## At a Glance

Your platform is down. The screen is white. The loading spinner turns forever. The AI agent that deployed the last change says: "Deployment successful. The castle is live."

The terminal says nothing. The console says nothing. The network tab shows requests that left and never came back. Everything looks normal except that nothing works.

This is a Blizzard.

---

## More Info

### What a Blizzard Is Not

A Blizzard is not a crash. Crashes are loud. A crash gives you a stack trace, a red error bar, a process that exited with code 1. You can read a crash. You can grep for it. You can paste it into a chat and say "fix this."

A Blizzard is not a Conk. A Conk is a discrete failure — a 403, a missing module, a build step that returns non-zero. A Conk has a location. You know where it happened and roughly why.

A Blizzard is **silence where there should be signal**. The app

That's not a failure of the reader — it's a failure of distribution. The paper demands you start at the beginning and commit to the end. The reader wants to touch the surface first and decide if the water is worth diving into.

Skipping Stones fixes this.

My daughter has teeth coming in. Which means the other teeth need to go out.

We explained to her that it would be a little bit of discomfort — brushing hard enough to be effective, wiggling the loose tooth a little, keeping at it a little at a time. Not because we enjoy watching a seven-year-old wince. Because she wants to eat on that side of her mouth without pain. And the only way to get there is through a little discomfort now.

She didn't love hearing it. But she understood it. Because the thing she wants — eating without pain, a smile that works — is worth more than the temporary sting of brushing where it hurts.

What does a career cost?

If you want to drive for Uber, you need a qualifying vehicle, commercial insurance ($150-300/month), and survival through an opaque algorithmic approval process. If you want to sell on Etsy, you need inventory, photography, and the tolerance for a platform that raised fees in 2022 while sellers had no mechanism to resist. If you want to teach on Udemy, you need to accept that 63% of your revenue disappears into the platform.

Everything above 150 is crowd.

The Lighthouse Ladder says yes.

## The Pudding

Every platform wants to know who you are.

LinkedIn asks for your job title. Uber asks for your driver's license. Most personality-based platforms hand you a test — 60 questions, a timer, and a label at the end that supposedly explains everything. Guardian. INTJ. Type A. Like a horoscope with a citation.

Liana Banyan does not give you a test.

# Your Money, Your Choice

## Three Options

When you fund a project, you select one of three options:

**Option A — Gift Receipt.** Your contribution is a gift. You expect nothing in return. Simple, clean, no strings.

**Option B — Credits Election.** Your contribution converts to Credits at $1 = 1 Credit. Those Credits live in your account and can be spent on goods and services within the cooperative. This is not an investment. Credits do not appreciate. They are prepaid service access.

**Option C — Community Fund.** Your contribution goes to the cooperative's community fund. It supports the platform's mission bro

*Time is a platform primitive. On most platforms, only the operator touches it. That's not neutral — that's a monopoly.*

---

## The Pudding

Every content feed you scroll is a scheduling decision someone else made for you. Facebook decided when to show you that post. TikTok decided when to interrupt you. Your email inbox decided when that newsletter arrived. The common thread: an operator picked the moment, and you received it.

This is so normal we rarely name it. Yet scheduling is one of the most powerful primitives a platform owns. When a platform monopolizes scheduling, members don't just lose control of *what* they see — they lose control of *when*. And "when" is the thing that turns information into practice. A good idea read on the commute is not the same as a good idea read at your desk with a notebook open.

### Bishop B076 | April 4, 2026 | Source: Canonical Basics

## Skipping Stone

*Cost + 20%. Locked. $500 → $416.67 to the creator, 83.3% of the value, every transaction, always. Not policy. Bylaw.*

---

## The Pudding

Most people learn about a platform by using it and then discovering the economics after they're locked in. Liana Banyan does it in reverse — the economics come first, and everything else is consequence.

**The mechanism is Cost + 20%.** On every transaction — a product sale, a service, a music license through Jukebox, an hour of HR help from Harper Guild — the platform takes Cost + 20%, and the worker keeps the rest. On a $500 transaction the creator keeps $416.67. That is 83.3% of the value to the person who did the work. The platform's 16.7% funds shared infrastructure and sixteen community initiatives.

# Ghost World: Your Digital Storefront

## What Is Ghost World?

Ghost World is the visual layer of the cooperative economy. Think of it as a map made of hexagonal tiles, where each tile represents a real member, business, or service.

Your storefront sits on your home island. Nearby tiles show other members and businesses in your area. The closer they are on the map, the closer they are in real life.

It is not a game. It is a visual way to discover what your community has to offer.

---

## The Pudding

# Try Before You Join

## How It Works

When you enter a contest or complete a challenge on the platform, you earn Marks. If you are not a member yet, those Marks go into a Guest Wallet tied to your email address.

Your Guest Wallet holds your Marks for 90 days. During that time, you can see what you have earned, but you cannot spend them yet.

When you decide to join (it is $5 per year), your Guest Wallet balance transfers to your member account instantly. Every Mark you earned before joining is yours.

---

# The LB Card: Your Cooperative Wallet

## How It Works

Your LB Card is a Stripe-powered prepaid card tied to your membership. You load Credits onto it (remember: $1 = 1 Credit, one-way — Credits never convert back to dollars). Then you use those Credits at participating restaurants, shops, and service providers.

When you spend 10 Credits at a local restaurant through the cooperative:
- The restaurant gets 8.33 Credits (83.3%)
- The platform gets 1.67 Credits (Cost+20%)
- The restaurant pays less than they pay DoorDash or UberEats
- You pay less than retail because of cooperative volume pricing

title: PUDDING #154 — "The Two Shells"

Every platform you've ever used makes you do two things at once: figure out what it IS and figure out how to USE it.

That's because most platforms have one shell. One frame. One interface that somehow has to serve the person who just arrived AND the person who's been there for three years. The result is a compromise nobody asked for: the new user sees too many buttons, and the veteran can't find anything because the buttons keep moving to make room for the "simplified" onboarding.

We solved this by building two shells.

We built a messaging system called Battery Dispatch that does something no other broadcasting tool does. Before you can type a single word, it asks you a question: *What change are you trying to make?*

That's the Intent Field. It sits above the composer. It's not optional. It's not a tooltip. It's not a "best practice" reminder that disappears after you dismiss it three times. It's a required field that gates access to the entire composition surface. You cannot draft a message on Liana Banyan without first declaring what you're trying to accomplish by sending it.

When a dispute happens on a platform, one of three things happens. A human moderator makes a call in a queue they're too overworked to think about. An algorithm makes a call nobody can explain. Or the platform makes no call at all and the louder party wins.

In all three cases, the reasoning is invisible. You get a verdict. You don't get to see how it was reached.

We built a courtroom with glass walls.

# PUDDING #158 — "The Four Judges"
## Bishop B082 | April 5, 2026
## Category: Governance | Tags: founder, kitchen, governance, ghost

## THE PROOF

When a dispute happens on a platform, one of three things happens. A human moderator makes a call in a queue they're too overworked to think about. An algorithm makes a call nobody can explain. Or the platform makes no call at all and the louder party wins.

In all three cases, the reasoning is invisible. You get a verdict. You don't get to see how it was reached.

We built a courtroom with glass walls.

---

## THE PUDDING

Liana Banyan's dispute resolution system is called Star Chamber. It runs four AI judges in parallel. Not one. Four. Each with a different reasoning profile, a different backend, and a different job.

**Oracle** runs on Claude. Oracle is principled and precedent-oriented. When Oracle evaluates a case, it looks for: what rule applies here? What happened last time? What's the consistent interpretation?

You would call that absurd. Work that has been completed does not un-complete itself. A meal you cooked was still cooked. A shed you built is still standing. A child you tutored still learned the lesson. Completed work is permanent. It exists in the world as a fact.

This is the principle behind what Liana Banyan calls The Ratchet.

Would you accept your own deal?

The Founder calls it the Montana Principle, and it comes from a newspaper route.

When you're thirteen years old and you deliver newspapers in Montana, you learn something about deals very quickly. The newspaper company pays you a fraction of a cent per paper. You wake up at four in the morning. You ride your bike in weather that wants to kill you. And the deal is: if a customer doesn't pay, you eat the cost. Not the newspaper company. You. The thirteen-year-old.

## The Pudding

Twenty percent.

That's the number that makes people flinch. When they hear that the cooperative takes twenty percent of every transaction, the reflex is immediate: "That's a lot. That's too much. I could keep that."

And you could. On a platform that offers nothing and charges nothing, you keep a hundred percent of your revenue. You also find your own customers, build your own website, handle your own payments, resolve your own disputes, write your own legal agreements, market your own products, and spend your evenings doing bookkeeping instead of building.

Twenty percent is not a fee. It's a company.

---

## The call

My son's car broke down a few days ago. Fuel pump, and something going on with the gas tank. He works. He needs the car to get to work. This is the thing about being an adult — the car isn't a toy, it's a *pathway*.

I called the shop I've worked with for years.

Here's what happened next. The shop sent a tow truck to pick up the car — they paid for it. They bought the parts — they paid for those too. They put a tech on it, and **today — the same day I am writing this essay — we picked up the car**. My son drove it home himself. The timing is not a rhetorical flourish. That is actually what happened, on the actual day you are reading these words for the first time.

## Who motivates the motivator?

There's a saying I've carried with me for a long time. I don't remember exactly where I picked it up — some of it's mine, some of it was handed to me, most of it is worn smooth from use:

> **"Who motivates the motivator? The motivator does."**

In the military we called this *opening a can of false enthusiasm*. You're cold, you're tired, you haven't slept, the next twelve hours are going to be worse than the last twelve, and someone has to stand up first. So you do. You *choose* to. Not because you feel it. Because you decided to feel it.

The older I get the more I think that's most of the game. Not talent. Not luck. Not even hard

I am giving away 80% of it because keeping it would make me less effective, and I'd rather be capable and effective than rich.

The decision had already been made by people I'd never spoken to, about a team I'd been training with for months, and I found out about it the same way everyone else did — by reading a piece of paper taped to a wall.

That's how most organizations communicate with the people they affect. The decisions are made in private. The people affected learn about them after the fact. And the explanation, if one comes at all, is a cleaned-up version of whatever actually happened — the version that makes the decision look inevitable instead of chosen.

# Pudding #190: The Mush Index

*The instrument that measures whether AI is amplifying you, or replacing you.*

---

## At a Glance

We have a husky. His name is Inuka.

Common consensus on huskies is to train them not to bark. We didn't do that. We taught Inuka to *speak on command*. Now he speaks only when spoken to. That's not suppression — that's integration. Resonation with direction, not a constrained entity.

That's how we treat AI in our cooperative.

But it's easy to *say* you treat AI that way. The hard part is *measuring* whether you actually do.

So we built an instrument. We call it **the Mush Index**. Sled-dog metaphor. Inuka *is* a husky. Sled dogs cross thousands of miles of difficult terrain pulling far heavier loads than any one dog could handle alone — but they can't do it without a musher. The musher can't do it without the dogs. The team is the multiplier. The Mush Index measures the team.

---

## More Info

### Three numbers, nested

The Mush Index is composite.

That's how we treat AI in our cooperative.

# Pudding #191 — The Proof Is Running

*Or: you're looking at the empirical evidence right now.*

**By Jonathan Jones** · Founder, Liana Banyan
*Bishop Session B100 · FULL DRAFT · ~2,000 words*

---

People ask me for proof that the ROM-First AI inference architecture works.

I tell them they're looking at it.

## The industry assumption

The AI industry operates on an assumption so universal that nobody checks it: AI problems are solved by more AI.

You have a multi-agent coordination problem? Add a supervisor AI. You have a hallucination problem? Add a critic AI. You have a consistency problem? Run three AIs in parallel and vote. Every layer of the stack gets another neural network, another frontier model call, another inference charge. The assumption is that intelligence is the solution to every problem, including the problems that intelligence creates.

The assumption is wrong. And the proof that it's wrong is not a paper or a projection or a slide deck. The proof is this platform.

# Pudding #192: The Living Pyramid of Roots

*How a cooperative grows its own knowledge architecture from the ground up — literally.*

---

## At a Glance

If you give an AI a 65-gigabyte folder of mixed material — letters, articles, code, drafts, papers, project notes, voice transcripts — and ask it "organize this," it will usually do one of two things wrong.

It will either flatten everything into a single search index (no structure, just a haystack), or it will impose a top-down taxonomy you didn't ask for and probably don't want.

We built a third option. We call it the **Living Pyramid of Roots**.

It works like this. We send agents we call **Miners** into the corpus. Each Miner specializes in one topic. As it reads, when it discovers a *new* topic — a category outside its current specialty — it doesn't expand. It splits. **Mitosis.** One half stays on the original topic; the other half becomes a new Miner-Scribe specialized in the new topic, with its own provenance ledger and its

# Pudding #193: They Do What IP Does — Pass It On, As a Filter

*Why intellectual property is a filter, not a wall, and what that means for cooperative-AI architecture.*

---

## At a Glance

Most people think of intellectual property as a wall. The patent is a wall around the invention. The copyright is a wall around the work. The trademark is a wall around the name. Walls keep things in.

That's not what IP actually does.

IP is a *filter*. It's what you pass on, to whom, under what conditions. The patent doesn't keep your invention in. It controls *who passes through* — who can use, who can transmit, who can license, who can build on. Every patent is already a filter. The default setting is closed; the inventor sets the conditions for opening.

When the founder of our cooperative said *"They do what IP does — pass it on, as a filter"*, he was naming a class of agents in our architecture: the **Sculptors**.

Sculptors don't write the IP. They don't own the IP. They *instantiate* the

Forty-three percent of every productive AI cognition moment in our substrate threads through a phrase the founder articulated.

Without those anchors, the substrate would lose its anchor-density. The human element is not decorative. It is *load-bearing at the substrate-statistical level*.

# Pudding #196: REF Staff — Read, Don't Re-write

*The architectural safety property that makes the cooperative's substrate trustworthy by construction.*

---

## At a Glance

When you give an AI access to your material, the standard worry is: *what if it changes something?*

Not "what if it summarizes badly" or "what if it hallucinates" — those are about output quality. The deeper worry is: *what if the AI modifies my files?* What if it overwrites the original? What if I can't tell what it changed? What if my source-of-truth gets corrupted?

This worry is justified. Many AI systems *can* write to source. The user has to trust the policy.

In our cooperative, the answer is different. We don't ask you to trust the policy. **We made it impossible for the policy to be violated by construction.**

We call our agents **REF Staff**. Reference. Or Referee. Never Re-writer.

---

Every agent in our architecture — Miners, Sculptors, Bloodhounds, Three Fat

*"This is Your World. Shape it, or Someone Else WILL."*

It's not specifically about AI. It's about agency, generally. But it's also exactly about AI, specifically — because the AI conversation is in the middle of a long stretch where most people are doing one of three things:

**Denying it.** "AI doesn't really work; the hype is overblown; this will pass."
**Fighting it.** "Ban AI from this space; refuse it categorically; treat use as contamination."
**Ignoring it.** "I just won't engage; let other people figure it out."

All three are the *not-shaping* responses. All three cede the shaping decision to whoever shows up and is willing to do it.

We chose the fourth path. **Shape it.** Decide how AI fits into our cooperative. Make those decisions expl

*A family of named agents that compose a cooperative AI architecture, and why naming them matters.*

---

## At a Glance

Most AI systems are described by what they do.

Ours are described by *who they are.*

We have **Miners** who prospect corpora. **Sculptors** who shape what Miners produce. **Bloodhounds** who scout terrain before the Miners arrive. **The Three Fates** who route queries to the right knowledge sources. **The Loom** who weaves domain-expert contributions into reasoning fabric. **The Tribunal** who verify reasoning quality in real time. **Cerberus** who reviews completed reasoning chains retrospectively. **Heralds** who proactively surface relevant material. **Guards** who enforce consent at every cross-cathedral boundary. **Nurses** who care for sessions. **The Seer** who reasons over the full Pyramid via pointer-resolution. **The Augur** who synthesizes across multiple Seers.

Eleven named agent classes. A coherent family. A **Pantheon.*

We ran a benchmark. Cold Perplexity (no help from us) on a sealed synthetic corpus: **0% accuracy**. Same Perplexity with our cooperative's substrate loaded: **80% accuracy**. Same machine. Same model. Same vendor. Different substrate.

That's an eighty-percentage-point lift. On the other corpus we tested (mixed-coverage), the lift was sixty-two points. Cross-universe, the floor of retrieval-failure dropped to **zero** — the machine never came up empty when the substrate was loaded.

wading_diving_in_layer:
# Pudding #199: The Cathedral Effect

*The empirical finding behind everything we've built — and why the human element is what makes it work.*

---

## At a Glance

We ran a benchmark. Cold Perplexity (no help from us) on a sealed synthetic corpus: **0% accuracy**. Same Perplexity with our cooperative's substrate loaded: **80% accuracy**. Same machine. Same model. Same vendor. Different substrate.

That's an eighty-percentage-point lift. On the other corpus we tested (mixed-coverage), the lift was sixty-two points. Cross-universe, the floor of retrieval-failure dropped to **zero** — the machine never came up empty when the substrate was loaded.

We call this **the Cathedral Effect**. It's the empirical finding behind everything else we've built.

The architecture that produces it is what we call our *Cathedral substrate*: a structured, provenance-traced, scope-filtered, pointer-indexed knowledge layer that wraps any AI vendor's model. Free. Open-source. Pip-installable. Replicable in f

The standard advice in software engineering is: deploy carefully. Test the small version first. Stage the small deploy. Run the canary. *Then* commit.

---

## At a Glance

The standard advice in software engineering is: deploy carefully. Test the small version first. Stage the small deploy. Run the canary. *Then* commit.

The canary is the metaphor: a small, disposable proxy you send into the dangerous territory first, so that when something goes wrong, you find out before the full operation breaks. The canary's death warns the miners. The canary saves lives.

In modern software, the canary is a tenth of your traffic, a small subset of your servers, a five-percent rollout. The principle holds: send the small version first; catch problems before they're large.

This sounds like the grown-up choice. It is widely treated as the obvious default.

Twenty-one thousand people in San Antonio organized their own rideshare network through Facebook groups. No app. No company. No safety net.

They call it Hood Uber.

A March 2026 news report documented what this looks like in practice: cheaper fares than Uber and Lyft, cash and Venmo payments, drivers working dawn to dark six days a week. One driver was shot over an unpaid fare. Others carry weapons for protection. There are no background checks, no insurance requirements, and no payment guarantees.

This is not a failure of the people involved. This is a failure of the platforms that were supposed to serve them.

Liana Banyan uses three currencies: Credits (dollars you spend), Marks (effort you've contributed), and Joules (surplus the cooperative saved). They all have equal value. They serve different purposes. And together, they make it possible to run a cooperative without venture capital.

Most platforms have one kind of money: dollars. You pay dollars, the platform takes a percentage, and the creator gets what's left. Simple. Also extractive.

Liana Banyan has three currencies because the cooperative needs to track three different things that dollars alone can't distinguish.

title: Pudding #33: A Billion Rows and Five Dollars
canonical_topic: Pudding #33: A Billion Rows and Five Dollars — ---

skipping_stones_layers.in_depth:
---

---

Every platform that handles money needs a ledger. Every Mark earned, every Credit spent, every attribution chain event, every ADAPT score measurement — it all gets recorded. At a thousand members, that's five and a half million rows per year. At a million members, five and a half billion.

Most platforms solve this by throwing money at the problem. Bigger servers. More replicas. Managed services that charge by the row. The bill scales with the data, and the data scales with the users, and eventually you're spending more on infrastructure than you earn from membership.

wading_diving_in_layer:
# Pudding #33: A Billion Rows and Five Dollars
## How a $5/Year Cooperative Plans for Planet-Scale Data

---

### At a Glance (~50 words)
At one million members, Liana Banyan's ledger tables will hold over five billion rows per year. The platform stores only metadata — no photos, no video, no large files — so the cost of storing a billion transactions is measured in gigabytes, not terabytes. Planning for that scale starts now.

title: Pudding #35: Design Democracy — The Vote Before the Build
canonical_topic: Pudding #35: Design Democracy — The Vote Before the Build — ---
founder_voice_quotes: demand is validated before production begins
the vote creates a natural quality filter

skipping_stones_layers.in_depth:
---

---

Traditional manufacturing works backwards. A company designs a product, manufactures thousands of units, and then hopes customers buy them. When they don't, the result is markdowns, waste, and write-offs. The entire system optimizes for prediction — and predictions are usually wrong.

Design Democracy reverses the sequence. A member or team submits a product design to the platform. The community reviews it, discusses it, and votes. Designs that earn enough support enter the production pipeline. Designs that don't are archived — not deleted, not punished, just not manufactured yet.

Housing cooperatives have a revenue problem. Properties cost money to maintain. Tenants need affordable rent. And the gap between what maintenance costs and what affordable rent covers has to come from somewhere.

Most housing organizations solve this with grants, government subsidies, or higher rents that push the definition of "affordable." The WaterWheel solves it with a revenue split that uses short-term rental income to directly subsidize the people who live in the property full-time.

title: Pudding #39: One Hundred Factories
canonical_topic: Pudding #39: One Hundred Factories — ---

---

Platforms love unlimited scale. More sellers. More drivers. More nodes. The logic is simple: more supply means lower prices, more coverage, more revenue. It also means a race to the bottom where quality erodes, commitment vanishes, and individual participants become disposable.

Pioneer Nodes reject that logic entirely. The cooperative's distributed manufacturing network has a hard cap of 100 nodes. Each node is a registered manufacturing station — a member with verified equipment (SLA printers, FDM printers, CNC routers, laser cutters, injection molders, or desktop manufacturing stations) who can produce physical goods endorsed by the community through Design Democracy.

title: Pudding #40: The Earn-Down
canonical_topic: Pudding #40: The Earn-Down — ---

---

The standard model for vehicle access has two options. Buy a car (expensive upfront, depreciating asset) or rent one (perpetual payments, zero equity). Neither works well for people with limited capital who need reliable transportation for work.

Local Wheels introduces a third option: the earn-down. A cooperative fleet vehicle is available for members to drive. Revenue from the driver's work — deliveries, rideshare, errands, whatever generates income through the platform — is split 20/80. Twenty percent goes to the cooperative to cover vehicle costs, insurance, maintenance, and the platform's operating margin. Eighty percent goes to the driver.

wading_diving_in_layer:

title: Pudding #42: The Five-Dollar Question
canonical_topic: Pudding #42: The Five-Dollar Question — ---

---

---

Every pricing decision in a platform reveals what the platform actually thinks about its users. A $0 price means the user is the product — their attention, data, and behavior are being sold to someone else. A $9.99/month price means the platform needs recurring revenue to cover costs that are often inflated by investor expectations. A $5/year price means something different entirely.

At $5/year, the cooperative cannot subsidize bloated infrastructure with membership fees. It cannot hire a sales team. It cannot run Super Bowl ads. It cannot build features that only exist to justify a higher price. Every system must be lean enough to operate within the margin that Cost+20% provides on actual transactions.

The AI productivity story has two chapters. Chapter one is the good news: organizations are finally seeing returns on AI investment. Productivity is up. Output per worker is climbing. The tools work.

Chapter two is the problem: the gains are concentrated. A small cohort of "power users" captures most of the productivity benefit. Workers aged 22-25 in AI-exposed occupations are experiencing significant employment declines. The technology raises the ceiling for some and lowers the floor for others.

A customer wants to know three things:
1. What is it?

No tokens were converted. No internal currency was exchanged. Cash came in, cash went out. The cooperative is not a bank. It is a checkout counter.

### The Rideshare Extraction Model

Rideshare companies like Uber and Lyft have a structural problem: the driver provides the most expensive piece of equipment — the car — and the company captures the margin from its use. The driver absorbs:

- Depreciation (the car loses value with every mile)
- Fuel
- Insurance
- Maintenance
- Wear and tear on a personal asset used for commercial purposes

The company provides: an app and a payment system. The company earns: 25-40% of every fare.

### Why Five Dollars

Not free. Not ten. Not "starting at." Five.

A cooperative that costs nothing attracts people who value it at nothing. Five dollars is the minimum viable commitment: enough to filter out bots and tire-kickers, low enough that no human being in the target market is excluded.

Liana Banyan's Guided Tour works differently. It is modeled on how a museum docent operates: walk visitors to six landmarks, explain what makes each one interesting, and then let them explore on their own.

The tour has a hard cap of six stops. Not because the platform only has six features — it has dozens of domains across 23 migrated systems — but because six is the threshold at which orientation becomes overwhelm. A docent who tries to show you every painting in the Louvre has failed at their job.

A single coffee shop buys 50 pounds of beans a month. The distributor charges full price. A coalition of twelve coffee shops buys 600 pounds a month. The distributor offers a volume discount. The savings go back to the shops, not to a middleman.

This is not a new idea. Group purchasing organizations — GPOs — have operated in healthcare, hospitality, and food service for decades. What is new is making the mechanism available to any collection of small businesses on a cooperative platform for $5 per year per member.

On every military installation in America, there is a parking lot — sometimes near the PX, sometimes near the barracks — where service members park vehicles they want to sell or lend. It is called the Lemon Lot. No dealership. No intermediary. Just a vehicle, a sign with a phone number, and trust between neighbors who serve together.

The Lemon Lot on Liana Banyan digitizes this. A member lists their vehicle. Another member rents it. The floor rate is $15 per day — a platform-enforced minimum that ensures no one undercuts themselves into unprofitability. The database enforces this with a CHECK constraint: `daily_rate >= 15`. If you try to list your car for $10 a day, the system will not let you.

A rideshare driver on a conventional platform picks up a passenger for a $25 fare. The platform takes 25-40%. The driver receives $15-$19. The passenger has no idea how much the driver actually earned, and the driver has no idea how the platform calculated its cut.

On a route where the driver's gas cost is $8, the rider pays $9.60. The driver keeps $8. The platform's share is $1.60. No surge pricing. No algorithmic fare manipulation. No mystery math.

title: Pudding #96 — The Librarian
canonical_topic: Pudding #96 — The Librarian — *An AI knowledge management system that remembers everything
definition: *An AI knowledge management system that remembers everything so the agents don't have to.*
founder_voice_quotes: An AI knowledge management system that remembers everything so the agents don't have to.

skipping_stones_layers.at_a_glance: *An AI knowledge management system that remembers everything so the agents don't have to.*
skipping_stones_layers.in_depth:
*An AI knowledge management system that remembers everything so the agents don't have to.*

---

AI agents have a memory problem. Each session starts fresh. The context window — however large — is finite. When a codebase has 580 tables, 139 edge functions, 470 pages, and 2,130 documented innovations, no single session can hold all of it. The agent that built the housing domain yesterday does not remember doing it today.

The Librarian solves this. It is a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server — a TypeScript application that indexes the entire platform and exposes 23 specialized tools for querying the knowledge base. Instead of reading every file at session start, an agent calls `brief_me` with a task description and receives a compact, task-scoped context package in approximately 600 words.

Every platform has a take rate. Uber takes 25-40%. Etsy takes 6.5% plus listing fees plus advertising fees. Amazon takes 8-45% depending on category. DoorDash takes 15-30% from restaurants. In each case, the take rate is set by the platform, adjusted by the platform, and optimized for the platform's revenue — not the seller's earnings.

Liana Banyan has a take rate too. It is Cost+20%. Three words. One rule. Applied universally to every transaction on the platform, from a $5 subscription to a $5,000 manufacturing order.

What does Cost+20% mean in practice? The platform calculates its actual cost to facilitate a transaction — payment processing, server infrastructure, bandwidth, support overhead — and adds exactly 20% margin. On a $500 transaction, if the platform's cost is $83.33, the platform keeps $83.33. The creator keeps $416.67. That is the 83.3% creator share.

skipping_stones_layers.in_depth:
*Why Credits never cash out to dollars — and why that is the most important design decision in the entire system.*

On every other platform with an internal currency, there is a cash-out button. Twitch bits can be converted to dollars. Roblox Robux can be converted to dollars. Even airline miles — the original platform currency — can be redeemed for cash-equivalent purchases. The conversion creates a speculative layer: people accumulate the currency not because they want to use it, but because they want to convert it later at a favorable rate.

wading_diving_in_layer:
# Pudding #98 — The One-Way Valve

*Why Credits never cash out to dollars — and why that is the most important design decision in the entire system.*

---

On every other platform with an internal currency, there is a cash-out button. Twitch bits can be converted to dollars. Roblox Robux can be converted to dollars. Even airline miles — the original platform currency — can be redeemed for cash-equivalent purchases. The conversion creates a speculative layer: people accumulate the currency not because they want to use it, but because they want to convert it later at a favorable rate.

Liana Banyan's Credits, Marks, and Joules are one-way. Dollars convert to Credits at a 1:1 rate. But Credits never convert back to dollars. Marks are earned through effort and cannot be purchased or redeemed for cash. Joules are surplus currency generated when the cooperative produces more value than it consumes — and they cannot be cashed out either.

founder_voice_quotes: Cooperative housing exchange where members share homes instead of paying hotels.

skipping_stones_layers.at_a_glance: *Cooperative housing exchange where members share homes instead of paying hotels.*
skipping_stones_layers.in_depth:
*Cooperative housing exchange where members share homes instead of paying hotels.*

---

Airbnb started as a simple idea: people have spare rooms, travelers need affordable places to stay, connect them. The platform's cut was modest at first. Then it grew. Service fees climbed to 14-20% of the booking total. Cleaning fees became a second price hidden until checkout. Dynamic pricing algorithms raised rates during peak demand. The hosts kept less. The guests paid more. The platform kept the difference.

Liana Banyan's Vacation Network returns to the original premise with one structural difference: the platform cannot increase its take. Cost+20% applies to every booking. On a $100/night stay, the host keeps $83.33. The platform takes $16.67. No cleaning fee surcharges. No dynamic pricing algo

# Pudding #99 — The Vacation Network

*Cooperative housing exchange where members share homes instead of paying hotels.*

---

Airbnb started as a simple idea: people have spare rooms, travelers need affordable places to stay, connect them. The platform's cut was modest at first. Then it grew. Service fees climbed to 14-20% of the booking total. Cleaning fees became a second price hidden until checkout. Dynamic pricing algorithms raised rates during peak demand. The hosts kept less. The guests paid more. The platform kept the difference.

Liana Banyan's Vacation Network returns to the original premise with one structural difference: the platform cannot increase its take. Cost+20% applies to every booking. On a $100/night stay, the host keeps $83.33. The platform takes $16.67. No cleaning fee surcharges. No dynamic pricing algorithms. No service fee that changes based on demand.

**Core argument:** Traditional content platforms spend millions on CDN and storage. LB spends nothing because the content lives on members' existing social media. The platform is a registrar (who made what, where it lives, who gets paid) — not a host.

wading_diving_in_layer:
# PUDDING ARTICLE OUTLINES — Innovations #2100-#2104
## 5 Articles for Cephas Publication
## Bishop B048

---

## ARTICLE 1: "Zero Storage, Full Income: The Content Economy That Costs Nothing to Run"

**Hook:** What if a platform could host 10 million photos and pay $1.25/month for storage?

**Core argument:** Traditional content platforms spend millions on CDN and storage. LB spends nothing because the content lives on members' existing social media. The platform is a registrar (who made what, where it lives, who gets paid) — not a host.

**Covers:** #2100 (Bounty Photography), #2101 (Pearl Diver), zero-storage metadata architecture

**Key stat:** 10 million content items = ~10 GB of metadata = $1.25/month. Same content on AWS S3 + CloudFront = $30,000-$50,000/year.

**Reader takeaway:** Platform cooperatives don't need VC infrastructure budgets to compete with Big Tech content platforms. They need smart architecture.

**Length:** ~1,500 words
**Cephas slug:** `pudding/zero-storage-fu

> "That's why we built a platform where the closet belongs to the worker."

**Body angle** (~1,200-1,600 words):

1. Open with Kinder's quote as diagnosis

3. The hidden assumption: the closet belongs to the employer. The computer belongs to the employer. The output belongs to the employer. The worker is a renter of a closet they don't own, doing work they don't own,
